title: Traction Channels
summary: Traction is quantitative evidence of real customer demand — and it comes from one of nineteen distinct channels. This chapter defines traction, explains why channel selection (not product quality) kills most startups, and introduces the three frameworks the rest of the book develops.

pretest.prompt: A founder tells you their startup has 'great traction.' Before reading on, list three things they could mean by that — and for each, decide whether it would actually prove the business is working. What's the pattern in which ones count and which ones don't?
pretest.modelAnswer: They might mean: (a) 'we got a lot of press / a warm reception at a demo day' — feels good, proves nothing, people love free stuff and applaud startups on reflex; (b) 'we have 50,000 signups' — better, but only matters if those signups convert to meaningful use or revenue; (c) 'weekly paid customers are growing 20% month-over-month' — this is actual traction. The pattern: traction has to be *measurable*, it has to reflect *real customer demand* (not promises, not applause, not vanity downloads), and the trend has to be *up and to the right*. Press, sentiment, and top-of-funnel signups are not traction unless they convert to the real currency of your business — paying users, active users, orders, whatever is load-bearing for your model.

lesson.markdown:
## The definition

Traction is **quantitative evidence of customer demand**. It's the part of your business that proves — with numbers — that real people want what you're building, badly enough to do something measurable about it.

The operative word is *quantitative*. 'People love it' is not traction. 'We had a great launch week' is not traction. Traction is a chart: users, revenue, orders, retention, active usage — some metric that matters for your specific business, plotted over time, visibly trending up.

## Why the definition is strict

Startups are hallucination-prone. Every founder's friends say the idea is great. Every demo day has polite applause. Every launch has a press hit. None of that is evidence — it's noise that feels like evidence, which is worse than no evidence at all because it can convince you to keep building the wrong thing.

Traction cuts through the noise by demanding a number. If weekly active users were 400 last month and they're 600 this month, that's signal no story can fabricate. If they were 400 last month and they're still 400 this month, no amount of positive reception changes that.

## The right metric depends on your business

The book talks about 'traction' in the abstract, but in practice you have to pick a specific metric for *your* business:

- **Consumer subscription product:** weekly paying subscribers.
- **Marketplace:** completed transactions per week.
- **B2B SaaS:** paid seats, or MRR.
- **Ad-supported content:** weekly active users + engagement time.
- **E-commerce:** repeat purchase rate and cohort LTV, not just first orders.

The wrong metric is usually the one that's easiest to move without moving the underlying business — 'total signups ever' is the classic. Pick the metric that, if it doubles, the business is meaningfully more real.

## Traction is staged

The traction you need today isn't the traction you need in 18 months. An early-stage startup proving a concept might need 100 weekly active users. The same startup 12 months later needs 10,000. The definition doesn't change — quantitative evidence of demand — but the magnitude required to unlock the next stage (next hire, next round, next market) does. This is why the book treats traction as something you *pursue continuously*, not a milestone you reach and leave behind.

workedExample.setup: Two founders pitch you on the same Tuesday. Aisha runs ClipCast, a podcast-clip-sharing tool. She says: 'We launched three months ago, got written up in The Verge, have 22,000 signups, and investors love us.' Darius runs BillSync, a receipt-reconciliation tool for bookkeepers. He says: 'We have 140 paying customers at $49/mo. Last month it was 95. The month before that, 60. Churn is 3%.'
workedExample.walkthrough:
Who has traction? Darius, clearly — even though his absolute numbers are smaller.

Walk the definition: *quantitative evidence of customer demand.*

Aisha's 22,000 signups look big, but they aren't customers — they're email addresses. The Verge write-up is the reason for the signups, not evidence of demand: those people signed up because a journalist told them to, not because they felt a pull toward the product. 'Investors love us' is sentiment, not traction. If you asked Aisha what percentage of those 22,000 signups logged in a second time, or paid, or invited a friend, she'd either dodge the question or give a number that reframes the story. Her chart, honestly plotted, is: huge launch spike, then flat or decaying usage.

Darius's numbers tell the opposite story. 60 → 95 → 140 is a measurable trend. They're *paying* — the most honest signal, because paying requires reaching for a wallet. Churn at 3% means the product retains. His chart is small but unambiguously up-and-to-the-right.

The decision: you invest in Darius, not Aisha — not because his business is bigger, but because his business has *proof*. Aisha has a hypothesis wrapped in good PR.

This is the move the traction definition forces you to make. Without it, Aisha's pitch reads as stronger (bigger numbers, more buzz). With it, the comparison reverses.

contrast.explanation: This concept defines *what* traction is (measurable demand). The next concept is about *why* it matters for startups specifically (Paul Graham: a startup is a company designed to grow fast; Peter Thiel: most startups die of distribution failure, not product failure). Don't conflate them. Knowing the definition is step one; understanding why startups — uniquely among businesses — live or die by it is step two.

flashcards.front: Define traction in one sentence.
flashcards.back: Quantitative evidence of real customer demand — a measurable metric that reflects how much your actual target users want your product, trending up over time.

flashcards.front: Traction is ______ evidence of customer demand — not press, not sentiment, not applause.
flashcards.back: quantitative — the defining feature is that it's a number you can plot over time.

flashcards.front: Which of these is NOT traction: (a) 60 → 95 → 140 paying customers over 3 months, (b) 22,000 signups from a press launch, (c) 3% monthly churn?
flashcards.back: (b) 22,000 signups from a press launch. Signups driven by press are a reaction to the press, not to the product. Without follow-through (retention, paid conversion, repeat use), they aren't evidence of demand. (a) and (c) are both genuine traction signals.

flashcards.front: Why is 'total signups ever' usually a bad traction metric?
flashcards.back: Because it's easy to move without moving the underlying business — it only grows and never shrinks, it doesn't distinguish a real user from someone who abandoned after one visit, and it lets you tell a rising story even when active use is flat or falling.

flashcards.front: Why is the right traction metric different for different businesses?
flashcards.back: Because different business models are 'real' along different dimensions. A subscription business is proven by recurring paying subscribers; a marketplace by completed transactions; a content business by active engagement. Pick the metric that, if it doubles, makes your specific business meaningfully more real.

scenarios.setup: A founder shares her Q3 deck with you. The 'Traction' slide reads: 'Featured in TechCrunch, Fast Company, and Wired. 300% increase in website visitors. Signed 4 LOIs with enterprise prospects.'
scenarios.question: Evaluate this slide against the definition of traction. What's missing, and what would you ask the founder to show you instead?
scenarios.modelAnswer: None of the three items on the slide is traction under the strict definition. Press hits are sentiment, not demand. Website visitor growth is top-of-funnel volume, which is upstream of demand but doesn't prove it — visitors can surge without anyone converting. LOIs (letters of intent) are promises, not actions; enterprise LOIs are especially cheap to give. What's missing is any metric showing that actual customers did an actual thing that costs them something: paid, adopted, retained, referred. Ask her for: weekly active usage (or whatever the use metric is for her product), conversion rate from visit → signup → paid, and retention curves for the cohort from 90 days ago. If any of those are flat or decaying despite the press, the real story is that the product has no pull; the press just sent traffic to a leaky bucket.
rubric: Identifies press hits as sentiment, not traction
Identifies visitor growth as upstream funnel, not demand
Identifies LOIs as promises, not actions
Asks for a specific measurable use/payment/retention metric
Mentions that press + flat retention = leaky bucket, not traction

scenarios.setup: You advise a pre-seed founder building a B2B SaaS tool. She asks: 'What's the minimum traction I need to raise a seed round?' She currently has 400 free signups and no paid customers.
scenarios.question: Reframe her question using the definition of traction. What metric should she actually be targeting, and why is 'number of signups' not the answer?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Her question implicitly treats traction as a number of signups, which is exactly the vanity metric the strict definition rules out. Reframe: 'What quantitative evidence of customer demand would make investors believe this business has pull?' For a B2B SaaS, the load-bearing metric is paying customers or MRR, plus a clear upward trend. A common seed-stage bar is roughly $10k–$25k MRR *growing* month-over-month, or equivalent committed paid pilots. 400 free signups could translate into that if 5% convert at $50/mo — but until they do, the 400 is just a list. So her actionable target isn't 'more signups,' it's 'get the first 20–50 paying customers and establish a growth rate.' That's the number that proves the business is real; the signup count is a leading indicator at best and a distraction at worst.
rubric: Rejects 'signups' as the right metric for this business
Identifies paid customers or MRR as the correct B2B SaaS traction metric
Emphasizes trend (growth rate) not just level
Gives a concrete ballpark target or conversion calculation
Frames the actionable target in terms of demand evidence, not surface numbers

teachback.prompt: In 60 seconds, explain to a technical co-founder what traction is and — crucially — what traction is NOT. Cover (1) the definition, (2) at least two things people commonly mistake for traction, (3) why the right traction metric is business-specific, and (4) what makes a metric 'load-bearing' vs. a vanity metric.
rubric: States the one-sentence definition (quantitative evidence of customer demand)
Names at least two common false-positives (press, sentiment, LOIs, total-signups-ever, one-time spikes)
Explains why metric choice is business-specific with at least one example
Articulates the load-bearing test: 'if this doubles, is the business meaningfully more real?'
Explanation is causal — doesn't just list items, explains why each matters

concepts.title: Why Traction Is the Core Startup Problem

pretest.prompt: Before reading on: why do most startups die? Write down your two best guesses. Then: for each guess, is the usual fix to work harder on the *product*, or to work harder on *something else*? What does your answer imply about where founders should spend their time?
pretest.modelAnswer: Most founders guess 'ran out of money' or 'built the wrong product.' Both are proximate causes; the deeper reason is usually distribution failure — nobody ever found out about the product, or the channels the founder knew couldn't reach the customers who'd want it. Peter Thiel's blunt version: startups fail because they have no distribution, not because they have no product. The implication is uncomfortable: most early-stage founders over-invest in product and under-invest in channels, because product feels like 'real work' and channels feel like marketing (often dismissed). If distribution is actually the bottleneck, you should be spending ~50% of your time on traction from day one, not 'after we ship.'

lesson.markdown:
## Startups are defined by growth

Paul Graham's definition: **a startup is a company designed to grow fast**. Not a small business, not a new business — one whose existence depends on growth. Take away rapid growth and you don't have a slow-growth startup; you have a startup that's dying.

That definition has a consequence most founders miss: the thing that makes or breaks a startup is its growth engine, which means its ability to reliably acquire customers. Growth isn't a nice outcome of a good product — it's the *requirement* without which the entity isn't a startup.

## The distribution problem

Peter Thiel — investor, PayPal co-founder, author of *Zero to One* — gives the sharp form: **most startups die from distribution failure, not product failure**. They build something, can't get enough of the right people to try it, run out of money, and shut down. Post-mortems usually blame 'bad timing' or 'wrong market' but the mechanism is almost always: we never figured out how to reach customers at a cost we could afford, fast enough to matter.

This is counterintuitive because product failure is *visible* (the product was buggy, confusing, missing features) while distribution failure is *invisible* (the missing users never showed up to complain). Founders look at their product and see its flaws and conclude that's the problem. They look at their distribution and see... nothing, because there are no users. The absence doesn't self-advertise.

## The 50% rule

The book's prescriptive implication: spend roughly half your time on traction from the very beginning — not after launch, not once product is 'ready.' This sounds excessive to most engineering founders, who feel that time spent on marketing is time stolen from the product. But if distribution is where most startups die, time spent on distribution is *the highest-leverage time available*.

Half your time doesn't mean half your company. A two-person startup with a technical founder and a business founder might look balanced, but the technical founder still needs to spend meaningful time thinking about channels, because:

- **Product and channel co-evolve.** The product that works in SEO-heavy distribution is different from the product that works in outbound sales. Designing without channel in mind usually means building something that's a poor fit for any channel.
- **Channel experiments teach you about customers.** Running a cheap ad or sales call surfaces feedback you won't get from roadmap debates.

## The reframe

The old mental model: *build great product → customers come.* The Traction mental model: *customers come through specific channels → you must work those channels as hard as you work the product.* Every founding team has to absorb this reframe or risk joining the majority that shipped a lovely product nobody used.

workedExample.setup: Jun is the technical co-founder of Graphly, a data-viz tool for academic researchers. He's been heads-down for nine months on the product — rewriting the rendering engine, adding export formats. His co-founder Priya has been doing 'whatever came up': support, one-off sales calls, trying a few Google Ads that didn't seem to work. Graphly has 900 free signups, 14 paying users, revenue of $560/mo. Runway is six months.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Jun's mental model is the common one: *once the product is strong enough, users will convert.* He points to the rendering engine as the blocker. Priya disagrees but can't quite articulate why.

Apply the Thiel frame: is Graphly dying of product failure or distribution failure?

Evidence for distribution failure: 900 signups is not nothing — people are finding the site somehow. 14 paid is a 1.5% conversion, which is real but thin. More telling: Graphly has no systematic channel. 'Whatever came up' and 'a few Google Ads' is the classic symptom of a team that hasn't chosen a channel, isn't measuring one, and isn't learning. Researchers — their target users — cluster in specific places (academic Twitter, arXiv announcements, university mailing lists, conference poster sessions, methods-focused newsletters). Graphly hasn't tried any of those systematically.

Evidence for product failure: the rendering engine is slow, but the paying users aren't leaving over it (churn is fine). The product is imperfect but not the bottleneck.

Diagnosis: Graphly is dying of distribution failure. Jun is about to spend his last six months of runway on the rendering engine, which will make the product slightly better but won't change the fact that there's no reliable channel. Priya's unease is correct.

Prescription, applying the 50% rule: Jun takes two days a week to work on channels alongside Priya — specifically to run small tests in 2–3 academic-researcher-reachable channels (e.g., a targeted newsletter sponsorship, a methods-focused blog post series, a cold-outbound sequence to corresponding authors of relevant papers). He keeps three days a week on product, but only on things that unblock conversion (pricing page, onboarding flow, 'first chart in 60 seconds' experience). In three months, Graphly knows whether any channel works. In nine months of pure engine work, it would have known nothing new except its own runway date.

contrast.explanation: The previous concept defines traction (what it is). This concept explains why it's load-bearing for startups specifically — Graham's growth definition + Thiel's distribution-failure observation. Founders often intellectually accept the definition while emotionally still believing 'product quality is the core bottleneck.' Don't collapse the two: knowing what traction is doesn't change behavior; internalizing that *distribution is usually the killer* does.

flashcards.front: Paul Graham's definition of a startup — and why it matters for traction.
flashcards.back: A startup is a company designed to grow fast. The implication: growth isn't a nice outcome, it's the defining feature. Without rapid growth you don't have a slow startup, you have a dying one — so the growth engine (traction channels) is load-bearing, not optional.

flashcards.front: Peter Thiel's claim about how most startups die.
flashcards.back: Most startups die from distribution failure, not product failure. They couldn't reach enough of the right customers at an affordable cost, fast enough, before running out of money. Product flaws are visible; distribution failure is invisible (the missing users don't show up to complain).

flashcards.front: The book's prescriptive rule: spend roughly ______ of your time on traction, starting from ______ (not after launch).
flashcards.back: half (50%) ; day one — because distribution is usually the bottleneck, time spent there is the highest-leverage time available.

flashcards.front: Why is distribution failure harder to notice than product failure?
flashcards.back: Because it's invisible. Product flaws get reported by users who show up. Distribution failure consists of users who never showed up — their absence doesn't generate complaints, bug reports, or support tickets, so founders fixate on visible product issues and miss the real bottleneck.

flashcards.front: Give two reasons the technical founder (not just the business founder) needs to spend meaningful time on channels.
flashcards.back: (1) Product and channel co-evolve — the product that fits SEO distribution looks different from one that fits outbound sales, so channel-blind product design produces poor channel fit. (2) Channel experiments surface customer feedback roadmap discussions can't — a cold call or cheap ad teaches you things you won't learn from internal debates.

scenarios.setup: A technical founder, nine months in, has a polished product and 40 paying users at $20/mo. Growth has been roughly flat for three months. He tells you: 'The issue is that we need better onboarding and more integrations — once those ship, growth will unlock.' Runway: five months.
scenarios.question: Is this diagnosis more likely product failure or distribution failure? How would you stress-test his claim?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Probably distribution failure masquerading as product failure. Flat growth at 40 users is rarely an onboarding problem — it's a 'not enough people are arriving at the door' problem. Onboarding affects conversion *given* traffic; if traffic is flat, better onboarding moves the number from 40 to maybe 55. Stress-test: ask him for (a) weekly new visitors, (b) visitor → signup rate, (c) signup → paid rate, (d) churn. If visitors are flat at low numbers, onboarding isn't the bottleneck — the top of the funnel is. If visitors are fine but signup rate is terrible, then onboarding might be real. Ask him: 'Which channel is supposed to bring the users that better onboarding will convert?' If he can't name a specific channel with a testable hypothesis, he's confusing product-polish with distribution, and he's five months from a shutdown.
rubric: Diagnoses the situation as likely distribution failure, not product failure
Distinguishes top-of-funnel (traffic) from conversion (onboarding)
Asks for specific funnel metrics to confirm the diagnosis
Poses the 'which channel is supposed to bring these users?' test
Notes the runway pressure makes misdiagnosis especially costly

scenarios.setup: A two-person team pushes back on the 50% rule: 'We only have two engineers. If we each spend half our time on marketing, the product will stagnate. We'll do traction after we raise the A round.'
scenarios.question: Respond to this pushback using Graham and Thiel's framing. Where is the flaw in the 'traction after the A' plan?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The flaw: there won't be an A round without traction, so 'traction after the A' is logically impossible. Investors at the A stage are specifically buying evidence of growth — exactly the traction this founder is deferring. Graham's point applies directly: a company without growth is not a startup, and a startup in the zero-growth phase is burning runway on the hope that 'later' they'll solve a problem whose solution requires time and experimentation they're choosing not to invest in. Thiel's point is the second punch: most of those companies then die of distribution failure because they never built the channel, not because they never built the product. The honest reframe: you can't afford *not* to spend 50% on traction, because traction is the gating resource for everything else (next round, next hire, next runway extension). A better plan: pick one channel to seriously test this quarter, keep engineering moving on conversion-critical product work only, and let non-critical product work slip. Product purity on zero growth is not survival.
rubric: Identifies the logical loop: 'traction after the A' is circular because the A requires traction
Uses Graham's growth-definition explicitly
Uses Thiel's distribution-failure framing explicitly
Suggests a concrete redistribution (e.g., one channel test + conversion-critical product only)
Rejects the 'product purity' frame as a path to shutdown

teachback.prompt: Explain to a skeptical engineer why traction (not product quality) is usually the thing that kills startups. Cover: (1) Graham's definition of a startup, (2) Thiel's distribution-failure claim and why it's invisible, (3) the 50% rule and why it applies from day one, and (4) why even technical founders need to be involved in channel decisions.
rubric: States Graham's growth-definition in the engineer's own words
States Thiel's distribution-failure claim and explains the invisibility mechanism
Names the 50% time rule and its 'from day one' caveat
Gives at least one reason technical founders specifically must engage (product-channel coevolution OR customer-learning)
Explanation is causal — explains why, not just what

pretest.prompt: Before reading: how many distinct *categories* of ways are there to acquire customers? Try to list as many as you can in 60 seconds without looking anything up. When you're done, count them and note which ones you listed easily vs. which required effort. What does that distribution tell you about your own biases?
pretest.modelAnswer: Most founders list 6–10 categories easily: SEO, paid ads, content, social media, email, PR, sales, referrals, maybe events. They struggle past that. The book's claim is that there are nineteen — meaning the average founder is blind to roughly half the options. The distribution of easy-vs-effortful items maps your familiarity bias directly: whatever rolled off quickly is where you'd default-bet, and whatever you couldn't name is where you have zero intuition (and therefore zero evidence either way). The value of having a full, enumerated list is that it forces you past your own blind spots: if you can't evaluate a channel you don't know exists, you can't choose it, and you certainly can't rule it out.

lesson.markdown:
## A finite, enumerable set

The first load-bearing claim of the book: there are **nineteen distinct traction channels**, and every high-growth company got there through one of them. Not approximately nineteen. Nineteen. The set is treated as closed — an exhaustive taxonomy, not an incomplete survey.

That bounded number is doing real work pedagogically. A closed list can be *scanned* — you can consider each one in turn and ask 'does this fit my business?'. An open-ended 'think creatively about growth' prompt cannot. The whole Bullseye process in Chapter 2 is only possible because this list is finite.

## The 19 channels

1. **Targeting Blogs** — getting coverage or sponsorship in blogs your audience already reads.
2. **Publicity (PR)** — traditional press, news coverage.
3. **Unconventional PR** — stunts, gifts, memorable moments that generate coverage.
4. **Search Engine Marketing (SEM)** — paid search ads (e.g., Google Ads).
5. **Social and Display Ads** — paid ads on social networks and ad networks.
6. **Offline Ads** — TV, radio, print, billboards, direct mail.
7. **Search Engine Optimization (SEO)** — organic search ranking.
8. **Content Marketing** — a blog or similar owned channel that compounds.
9. **Email Marketing** — lifecycle and outbound email to lists you build or acquire.
10. **Engineering as Marketing** — free tools, calculators, widgets that generate leads.
11. **Viral Marketing** — product mechanics that cause users to invite users.
12. **Business Development (BD)** — strategic partnerships with other companies.
13. **Sales** — direct outreach by humans, especially in higher-ACV B2B.
14. **Affiliate Programs** — third parties paid on conversion.
15. **Existing Platforms** — building on top of App Stores, browser extensions, marketplaces with traffic.
16. **Trade Shows** — industry-specific in-person events.
17. **Offline Events** — meetups, conferences, your own events.
18. **Speaking Engagements** — talks at conferences and podcasts.
19. **Community Building** — cultivating a group of users who advocate and recruit for you.

## Each channel is a different world

These are not interchangeable. Each channel has its own mechanics, its own cost curves, its own ceiling, its own expertise requirements, and its own customer profile. 'Content marketing' and 'Sales' both bring customers in, but the operational playbooks share almost nothing. The skills that make you great at SEO won't help you run a trade-show strategy.

This has an implication: you can't learn all channels. You don't need to. Chapter 2's Bullseye framework is how you *select* the channel to go deep on at your current stage, without knowing all nineteen deeply.

## Why enumeration matters

Founders who don't know the list tend to operate with a mental list of 6–10. They're blindest to channels that don't *feel* like marketing to them: engineering-as-marketing feels like a 'product project,' community building feels like 'being nice on Slack,' speaking engagements feel like 'personal branding.' Each of these has produced 8–9 figure outcomes for specific companies. The enumerated list forces you to look at each one as a legitimate option — including the ones your instinct dismisses.

workedExample.setup: Theo is launching Pipewatch, a monitoring tool for plumbing contractors ($79/mo, B2B SaaS, US-only, target market ~200k plumbing businesses). Asked to list traction channels he could try, he says: 'Google Ads, Facebook Ads, content marketing, cold email, and maybe sales calls.' Five channels.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Theo's list is the symptom the enumeration is designed to fix. Walk through the 19 and see what he missed:

- *Targeting Blogs* — plumbing-industry publications and blogs exist (PHC News, Plumbing & Mechanical). Theo didn't list them.
- *PR* — trade-press features about a tool solving a real contractor pain are very achievable; not on his list.
- *SEO* — he has 'content marketing' but not 'SEO,' which is a distinct channel with distinct mechanics (technical ranking vs. publishing for authority).
- *Engineering as Marketing* — a free 'plumbing code lookup by zip' tool could drive enormous inbound; Theo didn't consider it because it doesn't feel like marketing.
- *Trade Shows* — the plumbing industry has dozens per year (AHR Expo, PHCC Connect). Not on his list.
- *Speaking Engagements* — plumbing-association podcasts and conference talks. Not considered.
- *Community Building* — Reddit's r/Plumbing and several Facebook groups have tens of thousands of active contractors. Not considered.
- *BD / Partnerships* — wholesalers like Ferguson and Grainger reach every plumber in the US; a partnership with even one is leverage. Not considered.
- *Existing Platforms* — QuickBooks' app marketplace is where SMB service businesses shop for add-ons. Not considered.

Of his five listed, two (SEM and Facebook Ads) are the two channels plumbing contractors are least active on as buyers (they don't respond well to paid social, and SEM CPCs in the trades are often brutal). Of the ~10 he missed, at least three (trade shows, community building, engineering-as-marketing) fit his audience shape exceptionally well.

The fix isn't to use all 19 — it's to *consider* all 19, then choose. Theo's real starting set should include Trade Shows, Engineering as Marketing, Targeting Blogs, and BD, all of which were invisible to him before the enumeration forced them into view.

contrast.explanation: The 19 channels are a *taxonomy* — a list of categories. The brainstorm step in Bullseye (Ch 2) is the *process* of generating at least one idea per channel for your specific business. Don't confuse the two. Knowing the list is necessary but not sufficient; you still have to do the per-channel brainstorming work. Similarly, doing the brainstorming work without first knowing the list guarantees you skip channels.

flashcards.front: How many traction channels does the book enumerate, and why does a bounded number matter?
flashcards.back: Nineteen. Boundedness matters because a closed list can be systematically scanned channel-by-channel — the whole Bullseye selection process relies on being able to consider each one in turn. An open-ended 'think about growth' prompt cannot force coverage.

flashcards.front: Three traction channels founders most often skip because they don't *feel* like marketing: ______, ______, and ______.
flashcards.back: Engineering as Marketing (feels like a product project), Community Building (feels like being nice on Slack), Speaking Engagements (feels like personal branding) — each has driven major outcomes for specific companies.

flashcards.front: Why aren't the 19 channels interchangeable?
flashcards.back: Each has its own mechanics, cost curve, ceiling, required expertise, and customer profile. Skills in one rarely transfer. Content marketing and Sales both bring customers, but the operational playbooks share almost nothing.

flashcards.front: Which channel is 'free tools, calculators, and widgets that generate leads' — and what's its sneaky feature?
flashcards.back: Engineering as Marketing. Its sneaky feature is that most founders don't categorize it as marketing — they see it as a product side-project — so it often gets skipped despite being a high-leverage fit for technical teams (e.g., HubSpot's Website Grader, HaveIBeenPwned).

flashcards.front: SEO and Content Marketing both involve written content. Why are they distinct channels?
flashcards.back: They optimize for different things with different operational playbooks. SEO optimizes for ranking on specific queries (keyword research, technical SEO, link building). Content marketing optimizes for audience and trust (editorial quality, distribution, brand). A single blog post might serve either, but the two strategies, teams, and metrics look quite different.

scenarios.setup: A consumer app founder asks: 'We tried Facebook Ads, Google Ads, and Instagram influencers and none of them worked. Should we give up?'
scenarios.question: Using the 19-channels framing, what's wrong with this conclusion? What would you suggest next?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The founder has tested three channels — all of them variants of paid social/search — and concluded nothing works. Against 19 channels, that's 3/19 coverage, and the three are closely related (all paid top-of-funnel). The conclusion is premature by an order of magnitude. Suggest: list all 19 channels and note which haven't been tested. For a consumer app specifically, likely underexplored channels include Viral Marketing (product mechanics that drive invites), Existing Platforms (App Store featuring, TikTok, Reddit communities), Engineering as Marketing (a free tool or widget that brings people in), PR/Unconventional PR, Community Building, and Content Marketing. Giving up after 3 closely-related paid channels is familiarity-bias failure, not 'nothing works.'
rubric: Notes that 3 channels tested = 16 channels not tested
Points out the 3 tested channels are closely related (paid acquisition family)
Names at least three specific underexplored channels with rationale
Frames the error as familiarity bias / incomplete coverage, not market failure
Recommends a full 19-channel scan before any 'give up' conclusion

scenarios.setup: A developer-tools founder says: 'Content marketing and SEO are basically the same thing for us — we write blog posts, we rank on Google, we get signups. Treating them as two channels is pedantic.'
scenarios.question: Is this conflation harmless, or does it matter? Defend your answer.
scenarios.modelAnswer: It matters — conflating them usually means neither gets executed well. SEO is driven by keyword research and technical ranking mechanics: pick high-intent queries, build topical authority, structure pages for crawlers and click-through. Content marketing is driven by audience building: editorial voice, distribution to a mailing list, repeat readers who trust you. A team doing 'both as one' typically writes posts that rank OK but don't build a loyal audience, or build audience but don't rank — because the two optimizations push toward different content shapes. Treating them as two distinct channels forces the team to say 'which are we actually running this cycle?' If the answer is SEO, the brief looks like 'optimize for rank on these 12 queries.' If the answer is content marketing, the brief looks like 'build a 3,000-subscriber newsletter with a consistent voice.' The distinction isn't pedantic; it's operationally load-bearing.
rubric: Articulates concrete differences in operational playbook (keyword research vs. editorial audience)
Names the failure mode of conflation (weak execution on both)
Explains why the distinction drives different briefs / KPIs
Rejects the 'pedantic' framing explicitly
Uses at least one concrete example of how the content itself differs

teachback.prompt: Explain to a non-marketer why the book enumerates exactly 19 channels (rather than, say, telling founders to 'think creatively about growth'). Cover: (1) what boundedness enables, (2) at least one example of a channel most founders skip and the company that crushed it with that channel, (3) why the channels aren't interchangeable, and (4) what you do *with* the list.
rubric: Explains that a closed list enables systematic coverage / scanning
Names at least one specific channel founders commonly skip, with a concrete company example
Explains non-interchangeability (different mechanics, skills, customers, ceilings)
Points forward to the Bullseye process as what you do with the list
Gives a concrete example of a founder's narrow default list (6–10) vs. the full 19

concepts.title: Familiarity Bias: The Channel You Know Isn't the Channel You Need

pretest.prompt: You're a founder with a strong background in one specific channel (say, SEO — you spent 8 years as an SEO consultant). You're starting a company. Before reading on: what's the *benefit* of your SEO expertise for the new company, and what's the *trap*? Be specific about the trap.
pretest.modelAnswer: Benefit: if SEO turns out to be the right channel for your company, you can execute faster, cheaper, and better than a typical founder. Your expertise is real leverage when it fits. Trap: you're likely to pick SEO *because it's familiar*, not because it's right. When you sit down to decide what to try, your brain gives SEO much more airtime than the other 18 channels — you can picture how an SEO plan would unfold in detail, while the alternatives feel abstract. That asymmetry in *imaginability* gets mistaken for an asymmetry in *plausibility*, and you end up defaulting to SEO for a business where (say) sales or community building is actually the right channel. The trap isn't that SEO is bad; it's that your familiarity distorts the comparison.

lesson.markdown:
## The default move

Asked to grow their business, most founders reach for a channel they already know. The developer-founder reaches for SEO and content. The salesperson-founder reaches for outbound. The ex-agency-owner reaches for paid ads. These are all reasonable-looking choices — they're rarely obviously wrong — but they're chosen for the wrong reason: *the founder's comfort*, not the business's fit.

This is **familiarity bias**, and it's the single most common failure mode in channel selection.

## Why familiarity feels like evidence

Familiarity bias is sneaky because it doesn't announce itself. When you imagine running a channel you know, you see a detailed, plausible picture: the ad copy, the keyword list, the sales script, the exact next step. When you imagine running a channel you don't know, you see a fuzzy outline. The contrast between a detailed plan and a fuzzy outline *feels like* evidence that the detailed plan is better — but it's only evidence that you know one better.

This is the imagination-vs-reality gap at work: mental fluency gets mistaken for strategic fit. The SEO consultant's SEO plan looks better than their hypothetical trade-show plan because they can actually draft it. That doesn't make SEO the right channel.

## The asymmetric cost

Familiarity bias has an asymmetric cost:

- **When familiar channel happens to fit:** you move faster (small upside — you'd have gotten to this channel eventually).
- **When familiar channel doesn't fit:** you spend months on it anyway, because it feels right, then conclude 'we tried marketing, nothing works' (large downside — you burn a big fraction of your runway on the wrong channel).

The expected cost is heavily negative because the downside case is both more common (you can only know one channel well; the other 18 are where the fit might actually be) and more expensive (months of runway vs. a few weeks saved on onboarding).

## The countermove

Familiarity bias is what the **brainstorm step** in Bullseye (Chapter 2) exists to counteract. The rule — at least one idea for every one of the 19 channels — is specifically designed to force you to consider the channels you'd otherwise dismiss. You still might end up picking the familiar channel; the difference is that it *earned* the pick by surviving comparison, rather than being default-selected.

A useful internal check: when you notice yourself gravitating toward a specific channel, ask 'would I still pick this if I had zero experience in it?' If the answer is 'probably not,' you're choosing with your bias, not your analysis.

## Familiarity bias isn't banned expertise

This isn't an argument against using expertise. If SEO genuinely is the right channel for your business, your SEO background is an enormous asset. The argument is against *letting expertise pick the channel for you*. Run the analysis. Let the channel pick itself on the merits. Then use your expertise to execute the winner better than anyone else could.

workedExample.setup: Renata spent 11 years as a B2B enterprise sales leader. She's now founding Vellum, a $19/mo consumer writing tool (note: consumer, not enterprise). Her default growth plan: 'I'll build a sales team and we'll close users.'
workedExample.walkthrough:
Pattern-match on familiarity bias: Renata is reaching for the channel she knows (sales) for a business shape that almost certainly doesn't fit it.

Stress-test the fit. Sales makes sense when:
- LTV is high enough to justify the cost of a salesperson ($5k+ typical minimum)
- Buyers are identifiable, reachable, and make deliberate purchase decisions
- Sales cycles are weeks-to-months and contract sizes are meaningful

Vellum is $19/mo consumer. Annual LTV even at great retention is maybe $150. A full-time salesperson costs $60–100k fully loaded; they'd need to close ~500 customers per year *per seller* to break even on their own comp, never mind profit. Consumer buyers don't take sales calls for $19 products — they click 'Start free trial' and decide in 30 seconds.

Renata's plan is an instance of familiarity bias in its purest form: the channel she knows is vivid to her, so it feels like the obvious answer. But the business mechanics rule it out. Her actual best-fit channels are probably Viral Marketing, Content Marketing, SEO (writing-tool keywords have huge volume), Community Building, or Targeting Blogs — none of which she has experience with, and all of which feel fuzzy to her compared to building a sales team.

The right move: run Bullseye, force one idea per channel, let the comparison happen on merits. Renata's 11 years of sales experience doesn't disappear — it just gets held until she's evaluating a product shape where sales is actually the right channel. For Vellum, it's not. Her instinct is expensive if she follows it.

contrast.explanation: Familiarity bias and unpredictability are two different reasons not to trust your instinct on channel selection. Familiarity bias says 'you'll pick the channel you know,' which is a bias about *your preferences*. Unpredictability says 'even without that bias, you can't reason your way to the right answer,' which is a claim about *the problem domain*. Together they motivate testing; individually, each one alone is insufficient (a bias-free but arrogant founder could still miss by reasoning, and a humble founder without the bias could still get lucky by instinct).

flashcards.front: Define familiarity bias in the context of channel selection.
flashcards.back: The tendency to pick a traction channel because you're personally comfortable with it (prior experience, mental fluency) rather than because it fits the business. Leads founders to default to channels matching their background, not channels matching their company.

flashcards.front: Familiarity bias confuses ______ with strategic fit — the channel you can picture in detail *feels* better than the channel you can only sketch vaguely.
flashcards.back: mental fluency (or imaginability) — detailed mental pictures are mistaken for superior plausibility; they only show that you know one channel better, not that it fits.

flashcards.front: The internal check question for catching familiarity bias in yourself.
flashcards.back: 'Would I still pick this channel if I had zero experience in it?' If no, you're letting familiarity drive the decision. The channel needs to earn its spot on fit, not on your comfort.

flashcards.front: Why is the *downside* case of familiarity bias much more costly than the upside case is valuable?
flashcards.back: Upside case (familiar channel happens to fit): small benefit — you'd have reached it anyway. Downside case (familiar channel doesn't fit): you spend months of runway on the wrong channel before concluding marketing 'doesn't work.' The expected value is strongly negative because the downside is both more common and more expensive.

flashcards.front: Does avoiding familiarity bias mean you shouldn't use your channel expertise?
flashcards.back: No. The goal is to not let expertise *pick* the channel. Run the analysis first; let the channel win on merits. If your familiar channel wins, your expertise is then a huge execution advantage. Expertise isn't banned — bias-driven channel selection is.

scenarios.setup: A founder with a PhD in machine learning — no marketing background — is starting a B2B analytics tool. She tells you: 'I don't know anything about marketing, so I'm going to hire a VP Marketing who has run paid acquisition at three SaaS companies. They'll pick the channel for us.'
scenarios.question: Is this plan immune to familiarity bias? Where might it go wrong?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Not immune — the bias is just transferred. The VP Marketing with three rounds of paid acquisition experience has their own familiarity bias: they'll almost certainly recommend paid. That might be right, but it'll be chosen for the VP's comfort, not the business's fit. The founder has swapped her own zero-channel bias for someone else's one-channel bias. The better plan: hire someone who can run the Bullseye process (or run it herself with an advisor). That means considering all 19 channels, ranking them, testing three in parallel. If paid wins on merits, fine — the VP's expertise then matters for execution. But the decision shouldn't come pre-loaded by the hire's resume.
rubric: Identifies that the VP also has familiarity bias (toward paid)
Recognizes that outsourcing the decision just moves the bias, doesn't remove it
Prescribes a Bullseye-style analysis before committing to a channel
Distinguishes channel-selection from channel-execution in framing the hire's role
Notes the VP's expertise is still valuable — just for execution, not decision

scenarios.setup: You're pair-working with a founder during their Bullseye brainstorm. They have 19 rows in a spreadsheet. Eleven rows are completed in detail; the other eight rows just say 'not relevant' or are blank.
scenarios.question: What's the failure mode, and what specifically should they do differently?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Classic familiarity-bias failure during brainstorm. 'Not relevant' for eight of nineteen channels is almost always 'I don't know this channel well enough to imagine using it' — not an actual judgment about fit. The whole point of forced coverage is to block that shortcut. Specifically: go back to each of the 8 blanks and spend 5–10 minutes researching what the channel looks like in similar companies (one analog company per channel), then draft one plausible idea even if the idea feels unlikely. You can downrank the channel at the *ranking* step, but not skip it at the brainstorm step. The goal is to evaluate all 19 on merits, and you cannot evaluate something you never considered.
rubric: Identifies that 'not relevant' on 8 channels is familiarity-driven dismissal, not analysis
Distinguishes brainstorm (generate) from rank (evaluate) — you don't skip at generate
Prescribes concrete research per channel (e.g., one analog company)
Requires at least one written idea per channel, even unlikely ones
Notes that downranking later is fine, but skipping now is the bias

teachback.prompt: Explain familiarity bias to a first-time founder. Cover: (1) what it is, (2) why it feels like good judgment rather than bias (the imagination-vs-reality gap), (3) the asymmetric cost, and (4) how Bullseye's brainstorm step counteracts it without banning expertise.
rubric: Defines familiarity bias clearly
Explains the mental-fluency-vs-fit confusion — detailed plans feel better because familiar, not because better
Notes the asymmetric cost (small upside, large downside)
Describes the countermove (forced coverage in brainstorm)
Clarifies that expertise isn't banned — just not allowed to pick the channel directly

concepts.title: You Can't Reason Your Way to the Right Channel

pretest.prompt: Suppose two experienced startup operators — both with great track records — predict which traction channel will work for a new business. How often would you expect them to agree? How often would you expect either of them to be right? What does that imply about how channel decisions should be made?
pretest.modelAnswer: Empirically: they agree surprisingly rarely, and whichever one is right is often right for reasons neither predicted. Channel performance depends on interactions between product, timing, market micro-structure, team, and luck — there are too many variables for intuition to reliably converge. The implication: if even two experts can't reason their way to the right answer, *you* certainly can't. Channel decisions therefore have to be made with cheap, fast, real-world evidence (testing) rather than a priori argument. The role of intuition and analysis is to narrow from 19 to 3, not to predict the winner outright.

lesson.markdown:
## The uncomfortable claim

The second load-bearing claim of the chapter: **you cannot reason your way to the right traction channel**. Even experienced operators — founders who've done this before, advisors with deep pattern-recognition — guess wrong at a rate that makes a priori prediction unreliable.

This is uncomfortable because founders *want* to figure things out by thinking. Thinking is free, testing costs money and time, and most technical founders are much better at thinking than testing. The wish is: 'if I just reason hard enough about our product, market, and customers, I'll know which channel will work.' The wish is unreliable.

## Why intuition fails here

Channel performance is the product of many interacting variables — product-market fit, timing, keyword saturation, platform quirks, competitor moves, content cycles, audience mood, pricing, brand — any of which can flip a channel from a winner to a loser. Human prediction does poorly when outcomes depend on many interacting variables, and channel outcomes are exactly that shape of problem.

Famous examples make the point:

- **Airbnb**: their early breakthrough came from Craigslist cross-posting — an integration channel nobody would have predicted as a growth engine.
- **Dropbox**: their referral program worked far better than all the paid acquisition they'd tried.
- **Mint**: pre-launch content marketing (the MintLife blog) delivered their first hundreds of thousands of users, ahead of any paid plan.

In each case, the winning channel wasn't obviously the best choice on paper before it was tested. It became obvious *after*. That's characteristic of unpredictable systems: clarity is available retrospectively, not in advance.

## What unpredictability implies

The operational consequence: channel decisions have to be evidence-driven, not argument-driven. Testing replaces guessing as the decision mechanism. This is why the book spends an entire framework (Bullseye) on a five-step loop whose core move is 'run three cheap tests in parallel and let the data pick the winner.'

The role of intuition shrinks but doesn't disappear. Intuition and analysis are good at:
- **Narrowing 19 to 3.** You can ballpark which channels are at least *plausibly* fit for your business, and rule out clear mismatches (e.g., offline billboards for a $5/mo consumer app).
- **Designing the tests.** Choosing what to actually try within each channel.
- **Reading the results.** Once data is in, interpreting what it means.

Intuition is bad at: pre-committing to one channel over another without testing. That's the failure mode.

## Why this matters

If you skip testing because you 'already know' which channel will win, you've made a bet against the historical base rate. Most founders who thought they knew were wrong. Even the ones who were right mostly got lucky — their confidence didn't predict their correctness. The safer move, the one that compounds into better outcomes across many decisions, is: narrow rationally, test cheaply, pick empirically.

workedExample.setup: Hassan is building Tempo, a $12/mo habit-tracking app. Before any testing, he's confident that 'social and display ads (Instagram specifically) will be our channel — habit apps are visual, our audience lives on Instagram, and we have gorgeous creative.' He wants to skip the parallel tests and put his entire $8k budget into Instagram ads immediately.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Hassan's argument is reasonable on its face. Habit apps *are* visual. Self-improvement audiences *are* on Instagram. Good creative *does* help. So why is his plan risky?

The risk isn't that Instagram is wrong — it might be right. The risk is that his reasoning has the structure of every founder who got their channel wrong. Consider what Hassan *doesn't* know:

- Whether his specific creative beats the benchmark CPM for the habit-tracking niche.
- Whether Instagram users who click habit-app ads convert to paid at a rate that makes CAC viable at $12/mo LTV.
- Whether his audience is actually more responsive on TikTok, where habit content is trending harder and CPMs are currently lower.
- Whether SEO on 'habit tracker app' queries (which have real volume and commercial intent) would produce a lower CAC with more retained users.
- Whether viral loops (habit-streak screenshots, friend-challenge invites) are available in his product — and if so, whether they dominate paid acquisition economics entirely.

Every one of those questions has a data answer. None of them have an intuition answer that's reliable.

If Hassan puts $8k into Instagram and CAC comes in at $40 (vs. his $12/mo, 18-month LTV of ~$180), he'll look at the result and say 'well, maybe more budget would work.' That's sunk-cost thinking — predictable after pre-committing. If instead he puts $1,500 into Instagram, $1,500 into TikTok, $1,500 into SEO, and $1,500 into building a viral-share feature, with clear pass/fail thresholds for each, within 4 weeks he knows which of the four actually works. That's the unpredictability-respecting move.

His strong intuition about Instagram isn't wasted — it puts Instagram in the inner-three. But it doesn't let him skip comparison. Three or four shots on goal beat one confident bet, every time the confident bet could be wrong — which, historically, is most of the time.

contrast.explanation: Unpredictability is the *reason* for cheap testing; cheap testing (Ch 2, Step 4) is the *response* to unpredictability. Don't just state 'we're running tests' — name why: because no amount of pre-test reasoning reliably identifies the winner. The mindset flows from the claim: if you believed you could reason to the answer, cheap testing would be wasted effort. Because you can't, it's the highest-leverage spend.

flashcards.front: State the unpredictability claim in one sentence.
flashcards.back: You cannot reliably reason your way to the right traction channel in advance — channel performance depends on too many interacting variables, so even experienced operators guess wrong at a high rate.

flashcards.front: Channel clarity is available ______, not ______. You see why a channel worked after it worked, rarely before.
flashcards.back: retrospectively ; prospectively (in advance) — this is characteristic of systems with many interacting variables, which is exactly what channel performance is.

flashcards.front: Name a famous startup whose winning channel would not have been predicted on paper, and what the channel was.
flashcards.back: Airbnb — Craigslist cross-posting (an integration hack). Dropbox — the referral program (outperformed paid acquisition). Mint — pre-launch content marketing (blog drove hundreds of thousands of first users). Any of these; the pattern is: winner identified via testing, not advance reasoning.

flashcards.front: What role does intuition still play, given unpredictability?
flashcards.back: Intuition is useful for narrowing 19 to 3, designing tests, and interpreting results. It's not reliable for picking a single winning channel in advance without evidence. Use intuition to shape the hypothesis space; use testing to decide within it.

flashcards.front: Why does pre-committing to one channel (skipping tests) tend to produce sunk-cost behavior later?
flashcards.back: Because after you've invested significant budget and time in the chosen channel, marginal results feel like 'almost working' — so the psychological response is to double down rather than kill. Pre-commitment creates the very condition (heavy investment) that makes honest evaluation harder. Cheap parallel tests avoid that trap.

scenarios.setup: A seasoned second-time founder (one prior exit) tells his team: 'I've been in B2B SaaS for 15 years. I *know* content marketing is our channel. Let's skip the Bullseye testing — it's for first-time founders who don't have pattern-matching yet.'
scenarios.question: Respond using the unpredictability principle. What's the flaw in appealing to experience here?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Experience narrows the plausibility set — a reasonable use — but it doesn't reliably pick the winning channel. Fifteen years of B2B SaaS means he's seen content work in some companies and fail in others; his current company's fit depends on its specific market, product, team, and timing, which his prior exit didn't test. Appealing to experience to *skip* testing inverts the relationship between experience and evidence. The correct move: use experience to prune aggressively (content marketing clearly gets inner-ring status; so probably does sales; so probably does targeted blogs for the SMB segment), then *still* run parallel tests on the 2-3 most plausible, because none of them is guaranteed to work. Fifteen years of pattern-matching is an input to Bullseye, not a replacement for it. The moment experience makes you confident enough to skip testing is the moment you're most likely to be wrong without finding out in time.
rubric: Distinguishes 'narrowing the space' from 'picking the winner' as uses of experience
Names the specific situational variables experience doesn't cover (market, product, timing)
Prescribes experience-as-input, not experience-as-replacement
Notes the danger: skipping testing = no feedback loop to catch errors
Rejects the 'Bullseye is for beginners' framing explicitly

scenarios.setup: A founder's parallel test results come back: Channel A delivered 30 signups at $50 CAC; Channel B delivered 8 signups at $20 CAC; Channel C delivered 0 signups. The founder says: 'Channel B has lower CAC, so B is the winner.'
scenarios.question: Is the CAC-only interpretation correct? What's the nuanced read?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Not necessarily. Low CAC with very low volume (8 signups) is weak evidence — the number is noisy, and the channel's ceiling might be tiny. Channel A delivered meaningful volume (30) at higher but not catastrophic CAC. The real question is which channel can *scale* — Channel A might produce 300 signups next month at similar CAC if the channel isn't saturated, while Channel B might cap out at 12 signups because its audience is minuscule. The right reading is: A is likely the winner; its higher CAC is acceptable if LTV supports it and the volume is scalable. B is worth a second small test to see if volume grows with more spend, but it's not clearly the winner just because CAC is lowest. C is a clean failure — park it. This is a case where intuition plus data together beats either alone: the data points to A, but only if you know to weight volume and scalability alongside CAC.
rubric: Rejects 'lowest CAC wins' as the rule
Names volume and scalability as co-equal criteria alongside CAC
Notes small-sample noise in Channel B's 8 signups
Distinguishes winner (A) from worth-retesting (B) from dead (C)
Frames the decision as data + judgment, not data alone

teachback.prompt: Explain the unpredictability principle to a senior engineer who wants to reason their way to the right channel. Cover: (1) the claim, (2) why intuition fails for this class of problem specifically, (3) a concrete example of a famous surprise-channel outcome, and (4) what role reasoning still plays given that you can't predict the winner.
rubric: States the claim clearly — can't reason to the answer
Explains *why* — many interacting variables, not a single-dimension problem
Gives a concrete example (Airbnb/Craigslist, Dropbox/referral, Mint/content, or equivalent)
Preserves a role for reasoning (narrowing, test design, interpretation)
Draws the practical implication: testing over arguing

concepts.title: The Three Frameworks the Book Gives You

pretest.prompt: Given what you now know (19 channels, familiarity bias, unpredictability, half your time on traction), what three *kinds of problems* would a founder still need help with — and what shape would each solution take? Think about the difference between 'which channel to bet on today,' 'how much traction focus is enough,' and 'which big bet is blocking all the others right now.'
pretest.modelAnswer: Three distinct problems. (1) *Selection*: of the 19 channels, which one should I be working on right now, at this stage, for this business? Needs a repeatable filtering process. (2) *Mindset/integration*: how do I make traction not a separate workstream but something woven into product, hiring, strategy? Needs a mental model that changes day-to-day behavior. (3) *Sequencing*: of all the things I could do to grow, which single thing, if unblocked, unlocks the most? Needs a way to identify the bottleneck. The three frameworks of the book — Bullseye, Traction Thinking, and the Critical Path — map onto those three problems respectively.

lesson.markdown:
## The roadmap

Chapter 1 sets up three problems. Chapters 2, 3, and 5 give you a framework for each. It's worth knowing the roadmap before you dive in, because each framework answers a specific question and they're easy to confuse.

## 1. Bullseye — which channel? (Ch 2)

**Question:** Of 19 possible traction channels, which one should you bet on at your current stage?

**Mechanism:** A five-step loop. Brainstorm (≥1 idea per channel) → Rank (sort channels into three concentric rings) → Prioritize (pick three inner-ring channels) → Test (run three cheap parallel experiments) → Focus (commit to the winner until it saturates, then rerun the loop).

**What it counteracts:** Familiarity bias (via forced coverage in brainstorm), unpredictability (via cheap parallel tests), and focus dilution (via the explicit cut to three).

**When you run it:** Every time you need a new growth push — typically when your current dominant channel is saturating or your stage has shifted.

## 2. Traction Thinking — what mindset? (Ch 3)

**Question:** How do you make traction a first-class concern across the whole company, not a siloed marketing workstream?

**Mechanism:** A set of mindset shifts — the 50% rule (half your time on traction from day one), treating product and traction as co-evolving, avoiding traction-vs-product as a false dichotomy, thinking in traction *goals* (specific numeric milestones), and running traction experiments continuously rather than in bursts.

**What it counteracts:** The common pathology where 'we'll focus on growth after the product is ready,' which produces well-built products with no distribution.

**When you use it:** Always. Unlike Bullseye, Traction Thinking isn't a process you run periodically — it's a perspective you carry continuously.

## 3. Critical Path — what next? (Ch 5)

**Question:** Of everything you could do, what's the *one* thing that, if you completed it, would most unblock your progress toward your next traction goal?

**Mechanism:** Define your next traction milestone (e.g., 'get to $50k MRR'), then work backwards to identify the shortest sequence of steps that reaches it. At any moment, you're working on the single most-upstream blocker in that sequence. Everything off the critical path is deferred.

**What it counteracts:** Diffuse effort — the tendency to work on many things that feel useful but aren't actually on the path to the next goal. Also counteracts premature optimization (polishing things off the critical path).

**When you use it:** When you have more to do than you can do, which is always.

## How they fit together

The three frameworks operate at different grains:

- **Traction Thinking** (always-on) = the *lens* through which you look at work.
- **Bullseye** (cyclical) = the *process* for picking the channel you're working on.
- **Critical Path** (continuous) = the *prioritization rule* for what you do today.

They don't conflict. Bullseye tells you your channel is (say) SEO. Critical Path tells you the one thing to ship this week to unblock SEO traction (publishing cluster content, say). Traction Thinking tells you not to treat either as a marketing sidetrack — they're the main event.

workedExample.setup: Imagine yourself as Priya from the Graphly example earlier. Six months from now, Graphly is at $12k MRR, growing 18%/mo. Priya and Jun want to triple revenue in the next six months. What does each of the three frameworks tell them to do — and how do they interact?
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Traction Thinking (mindset):** Priya reminds Jun that hitting $36k MRR isn't a marketing project — it's the company's central goal this quarter, and every engineering decision should be evaluated against it. When Jun proposes a 3-week rewrite of the rendering engine, Traction Thinking asks: does this rewrite move Graphly closer to $36k MRR, or is it yak-shaving? If it's not on the path, defer. This isn't a formal process — it's a constant background check.

**Bullseye (channel selection):** Their current channel is university-mailing-list sponsorships, which drove them from $4k to $12k MRR. But growth is starting to plateau — they've hit most of the high-value lists. Priya reruns the Bullseye loop. Brainstorm surfaces three new candidates: SEO (long-tail queries like 'academic poster graphic generator'), Engineering as Marketing (a free citation-graph tool they could ship), and BD (partnerships with two major reference-management tools). She puts all three in the inner circle and designs cheap tests — $2k total across them over 4 weeks.

**Critical Path (daily prioritization):** Given the $36k MRR goal and the Bullseye tests, what's the single most-upstream blocker right now? They identify it: the product's onboarding is losing 60% of mailing-list signups before they create a chart. If the new channels from Bullseye hit, that leak gets worse, not better. So the critical-path task this week isn't running the BD tests — it's fixing the onboarding. Bullseye tests are scheduled after onboarding is fixed, because otherwise any successful channel pours water into a leaky bucket.

Three frameworks, three distinct outputs: a lens, a channel hypothesis, a work queue. They aren't redundant; each answers a different question, and together they form a coherent growth plan that neither intuition nor any single framework would produce.

contrast.explanation: This concept is a *roadmap* to three frameworks; the Bullseye overview concept (Ch 2) is one of those frameworks in depth. Don't expect Chapter 1 to teach you *how* to run any of the three — it only positions them. If you want to actually execute, Chapter 2 gives you Bullseye, Chapter 3 gives you Traction Thinking, Chapter 5 gives you the Critical Path. Treat this concept as a mental table of contents, not an operating manual.

flashcards.front: Name the three frameworks and the question each answers.
flashcards.back: Bullseye (Ch 2): which of the 19 channels to bet on right now. Traction Thinking (Ch 3): what mindset keeps traction integrated with product rather than siloed. Critical Path (Ch 5): of everything you could do today, what one thing most unblocks your next traction goal.

flashcards.front: Bullseye is ______; Traction Thinking is ______; Critical Path is ______.
flashcards.back: cyclical (run when you need a new growth push) ; always-on (a continuous lens) ; continuous (daily prioritization rule)

flashcards.front: Which framework counteracts the pathology of 'we'll focus on growth after the product is ready'?
flashcards.back: Traction Thinking. It enforces the 50% rule (half your time on traction from day one) and treats product and distribution as co-evolving rather than sequential, specifically to block the 'product-first, growth-later' plan that kills most startups.

flashcards.front: The Critical Path framework — one-sentence mechanism.
flashcards.back: Define your next traction milestone, work backwards to the shortest sequence of steps that reaches it, and at any moment work only on the most-upstream unblocking step. Everything off the path is deferred.

flashcards.front: Bullseye says the channel is X. Critical Path says the task is Y. Do they conflict?
flashcards.back: No. Bullseye selects the channel to bet on at this stage; Critical Path selects the immediate next task that unblocks progress toward your next traction goal — which may be channel work, product work, or something else. They operate at different levels and are designed to compose.

scenarios.setup: A founder says: 'We've picked our channel (SEO) using Bullseye. Isn't that all the strategy we need? What's the Critical Path for?'
scenarios.question: Explain the role of the Critical Path given that the channel is already chosen. Give a concrete example of something Critical Path would catch that Bullseye wouldn't.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Bullseye answers 'which channel?' It doesn't answer 'what should we work on this week?' Even with SEO chosen, the founder has many possible SEO tasks (keyword research, technical SEO, content production, link building) *plus* product tasks that affect SEO conversion (landing page speed, onboarding flow, pricing page clarity). Critical Path adds the prioritization layer: of all the things we could do, which one most unblocks our next traction goal? Concrete example: if their SEO traffic is growing but converting poorly because the pricing page is confusing, the critical-path task is 'rewrite the pricing page,' not 'publish more SEO content.' Bullseye wouldn't catch that — it already picked SEO and doesn't descend into what's actually blocking growth today. Critical Path catches it because its question is specifically 'what's blocking the next milestone?'
rubric: Distinguishes channel selection (Bullseye) from within-channel prioritization (Critical Path)
Notes that Critical Path can prescribe product work even when the channel is chosen
Gives a concrete example where the bottleneck is off the obvious SEO task list
Explains Critical Path's core question (what's upstream of the next milestone?)
Avoids collapsing the two frameworks into one

scenarios.setup: A CTO objects: 'Traction Thinking sounds like management theater. We have Bullseye for picking channels and Critical Path for prioritization — why do we need a third thing that's just a mindset?'
scenarios.question: Defend or reject Traction Thinking's role. Is there a concrete failure mode that Bullseye + Critical Path alone wouldn't prevent?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Defend. The failure mode Traction Thinking specifically prevents: implicit downgrading of traction work when it conflicts with product work. Bullseye and Critical Path produce plans, but a team's *default assumption* about which work is 'real' shapes how those plans actually get executed. Without Traction Thinking as a lens, an engineer-led team runs Bullseye once, picks a channel, returns to product work, and quietly lets the channel deprioritize whenever a product task feels more urgent. Traction Thinking is the ambient norm — 'traction is co-equal with product, not a side project' — that keeps Bullseye's outputs from getting ignored and Critical Path's ordering from being silently rearranged. Concrete case: a new bug lands mid-week. Without Traction Thinking, the engineering team drops the SEO-improvement task to fix the bug ('product first'). With Traction Thinking, they ask: is this bug on the critical path to our next traction goal? If not, the bug gets filed, the SEO work continues. Mindset isn't theater — it determines what 'default' looks like, and defaults drive 80% of what actually gets done.
rubric: Defends Traction Thinking rather than dismissing it
Identifies the specific failure mode (implicit downgrading, product-first default)
Explains that Bullseye and Critical Path produce plans, but defaults determine execution
Gives a concrete 'bug lands mid-week' style example
Frames mindset as an ambient norm, not an additional process

teachback.prompt: In 90 seconds, give a new team member a map of the book's three frameworks. Cover: (1) the distinct question each answers, (2) the mechanism of each in one sentence, (3) the rhythm of use (when/how often), and (4) how they compose rather than conflict — ideally with a concrete walk-through of a decision that uses all three.
rubric: Names all three frameworks with the question each answers
Gives a one-sentence mechanism for each
Distinguishes the rhythms (Bullseye cyclical, Traction Thinking always-on, Critical Path continuous)
Shows how they compose — ideally a single example where each plays a role
Avoids conflating or collapsing the frameworks
